metadata.title: Why three graders read the same answer and hand back three different grades
metadata.topic: Each grader catches a different failure mode
metadata.purpose: Graders filter by different criteria — code catches structure, humans catch missed intent, model graders catch subtle semantic wrongness — so each surfaces a failure the others miss.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.visual_object: One output fanning into three grading pipelines, each emitting its own score
metadata.hook: You have one output and a simple question — is it correct? — and the answer depends entirely on which grader you ask.

beats.narration_text: Output "The capital of France is Paris, France." Code grader checks format → pass. Human grader sees the redundant "France, France" → marks it down. Model grader flags the unnecessary words → scores 0.8, not 1.0.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Why do different grading methods score the identical output differently?
You have one output and a simple question — is it correct? — and the answer depends entirely on which grader you ask.
Graders filter by different criteria — code catches structure, humans catch missed intent, model graders catch subtle semantic wrongness — so each surfaces a failure the others miss.
beats.visual: One output fanning into three grading pipelines, each emitting its own score
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Legal summary: code grader (word count OK), human lawyer (missed case law?), model grader (0.82 for tone).
Graders filter by different criteria — code catches structure, humans catch missed intent, model graders catch subtle semantic wrongness — so each surfaces a failure the others miss.
